stem: A 32-year-old G1P0 woman at 33 weeks and 2 days gestation attends day assessment with a two-day history of new frontal headache and intermittent right upper quadrant discomfort that has worsened over the last 24 hours. She denies visual disturbance, scotomata, abdominal pain in other regions, vaginal bleeding or reduced foetal movements. Her booking blood pressure at 12 weeks was 108/64 mmHg and all booking bloods (including renal, hepatic and platelet counts) were normal. Her dating ultrasound was reassuring and there is no personal or family history of hypertensive disease. On three repeated measurements 15 minutes apart today her blood pressure is 152/98 mmHg, 154/96 mmHg and 150/99 mmHg in the seated position with an appropriately sized cuff. A spot urinary protein/creatinine ratio is 18 mg/mmol, below the SOMANZ significant proteinuria threshold of 30 mg/mmol, and she has only trace blood on dipstick. The midwife notes hyperreflexia without clonus and asks the obstetric registrar whether the patient meets the diagnostic criteria for pre-eclampsia in the absence of significant proteinuria, given the bedside biochemistry shown below.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 152/98 mmHg (confirmed on three readings), HR 84 bpm, T 36.8 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Hyperreflexia bilaterally without clonus. Mild RUQ tenderness. Uterus soft, fundus appropriate for dates.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 118 g/L, platelets 92 x10^9/L, creatinine 82 micromol/L, ALT 78 U/L (ULN 35), AST 65 U/L (ULN 35), urate 0.38 mmol/L, urine PCR 18 mg/mmol. CTG normal.

lead_in: Which feature in this presentation independently satisfies the SOMANZ diagnostic criteria for pre-eclampsia in the absence of significant proteinuria?

options.text: Serum urate above the trimester-specific reference range value alone here
options.rationale: Hyperuricaemia is associated with pre-eclampsia and useful for prognosis but is not part of the SOMANZ diagnostic criteria for the disorder (SOMANZ 2014).
source_refs: SOMANZ Guideline for the management of hypertensive disorders of pregnancy

options.text: Hyperreflexia on clinical examination without any ankle clonus present today
options.rationale: Hyperreflexia is a clinical sign that raises suspicion of central nervous system involvement but is not a SOMANZ diagnostic criterion; eclampsia, persistent severe headache, visual scotomata or stroke are the neurological criteria (SOMANZ 2014).
source_refs: SOMANZ Guideline for the management of hypertensive disorders of pregnancy

options.text: Platelet count 92 x10^9/L with transaminases above twice the upper limit of normal
options.rationale: SOMANZ defines pre-eclampsia as new hypertension after 20 weeks with at least one maternal end-organ feature or uteroplacental dysfunction. Thrombocytopenia below 100 x10^9/L and transaminases more than twice the ULN are each independently diagnostic criteria (SOMANZ 2014; RANZCOG 2023). SOMANZ thrombocytopenia threshold is <100 x10^9/L; transaminase threshold is >2x ULN.
source_refs: SOMANZ Guideline for the management of hypertensive disorders of pregnancy

options.text: New right upper quadrant pain in clinical isolation without other features
options.rationale: Epigastric or RUQ pain raises suspicion for hepatic capsular stretch but is not a stand-alone SOMANZ diagnostic criterion without biochemical confirmation of hepatic dysfunction (SOMANZ 2014).
source_refs: SOMANZ Guideline for the management of hypertensive disorders of pregnancy

options.text: Urine protein/creatinine ratio of 18 mg/mmol on a spot sample
options.rationale: Significant proteinuria by SOMANZ requires a PCR of 30 mg/mmol or greater, or a 24-hour urine protein of 300 mg or more; 18 mg/mmol is below threshold (SOMANZ 2014).
source_refs: SOMANZ Guideline for the management of hypertensive disorders of pregnancy

explanation.summary: Pre-eclampsia is diagnosed when new hypertension after 20 weeks is accompanied by either significant proteinuria OR any maternal end-organ dysfunction (renal, hepatic, haematological, neurological, pulmonary oedema) OR uteroplacental dysfunction. Proteinuria is no longer mandatory.
key_points: SOMANZ end-organ criteria: platelets <100 x10^9/L, creatinine >90 micromol/L, ALT/AST >2x ULN, pulmonary oedema, eclampsia/severe headache/visual symptoms/stroke
Uteroplacental dysfunction: foetal growth restriction, abnormal Dopplers, stillbirth
Significant proteinuria: PCR >=30 mg/mmol or 24-hour >=300 mg
Hyperuricaemia is associated but not a diagnostic criterion
explanation.pearls: Always check platelets, creatinine and transaminases in any pregnant woman with new hypertension after 20 weeks; pre-eclampsia can present without proteinuria.

sources.label: SOMANZ Guideline for the management of hypertensive disorders of pregnancy

stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 woman at 35 weeks and 1 day gestation is admitted to the birth suite with severe pre-eclampsia. Her blood pressure is 168/112 mmHg confirmed on three measurements 15 minutes apart despite oral labetalol 200 mg taken two hours ago, she reports a brisk frontal headache and visual scotomata, platelets are 86 x10^9/L and ALT is 130 U/L (more than three times the upper limit of normal). There is no clonus and no focal neurology; reflexes are 3+. Renal function is normal with creatinine 68 micromol/L and urine output is 60 mL/h over the last four hours. CTG shows a reactive trace with no decelerations and the foetus is appropriately grown on recent ultrasound. The consultant decides to commence intravenous magnesium sulphate for seizure prophylaxis prior to expedited delivery, and asks the registrar to set up the SOMANZ/RANZCOG infusion regimen. The midwife asks specifically about the correct loading dose, the maintenance infusion rate, the monitoring required during the infusion, and what to do if a generalised tonic-clonic seizure occurs while the patient is already on the maintenance infusion.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 168/112 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, RR 16 /min, SpO2 98%.
data_table.Examination: Brisk reflexes 3+, no clonus. Chest clear. Uterus soft.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 112 g/L, platelets 86 x10^9/L, creatinine 68 micromol/L, ALT 130 U/L, AST 105 U/L, urate 0.42 mmol/L.

lead_in: Which magnesium sulphate regimen most closely matches Australian (SOMANZ/RANZCOG) recommendations for this patient?

options.text: Load 2 g IV over 20 minutes, then 0.5 g/h maintenance infusion, and 1 g IV bolus only if a seizure occurs
options.rationale: The load (2 g) and maintenance rate (0.5 g/h) are both subtherapeutic compared with the SOMANZ regimen (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Load 4 g IV over 15-20 minutes, then 1 g/h maintenance, with hourly monitoring of reflexes, respiratory rate and urine output; recurrent seizures treated with a further 2-4 g bolus
options.rationale: This matches the SOMANZ and RANZCOG regimen: 4 g IV loading dose over 15-20 minutes, 1 g/h maintenance infusion, hourly reflexes/RR/UO monitoring, and 2-4 g IV bolus for breakthrough seizures (SOMANZ 2014; RANZCOG 2023).

options.text: Load 6 g IV over 20 minutes, then 2 g/h maintenance infusion, with no monitoring required while urine output remains adequate hourly
options.rationale: The maintenance rate is double the recommended dose and reflex/respiratory monitoring is mandatory due to the narrow therapeutic window of magnesium (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Load 10 g IM (5 g into each buttock) without any IV loading dose, then 5 g IM every 4 hours as maintenance
options.rationale: The IM-only Pritchard regimen is used in low-resource settings; Australian practice uses the IV regimen which is more controllable and less painful (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Load 4 g IV over 5 minutes, then 1 g/h maintenance, ceasing the infusion if patellar reflexes are absent and giving calcium chloride only if respiratory rate falls below 12
options.rationale: The loading dose should be given over 15-20 minutes, not 5 minutes, to avoid hypotension and bradycardia. Absent reflexes signal toxicity and warrant calcium gluconate 1 g IV (SOMANZ 2014).

explanation.summary: SOMANZ/RANZCOG magnesium sulphate regimen for severe pre-eclampsia and eclampsia: 4 g IV loading over 15-20 minutes, then 1 g/h maintenance. Monitor reflexes, RR and UO hourly. Treat recurrent seizures with a further 2-4 g IV bolus. Calcium gluconate 1 g IV is the antidote for toxicity.
key_points: Loading dose: 4 g IV over 15-20 min in 100 mL saline
Maintenance: 1 g/h IV (reduce if renal impairment)
Recurrent seizure: further 2-4 g IV bolus over 5 minutes
Toxicity signs: loss of reflexes, RR <12, UO <100 mL/4 h
Antidote: calcium gluconate 1 g (10 mL of 10%) IV slow
explanation.pearls: Magnesium reduces eclampsia by half compared with placebo and is more effective than phenytoin or diazepam (Magpie trial; Cochrane).

stem: A 26-year-old G1P0 woman at 37 weeks and 4 days gestation, previously well with an uncomplicated pregnancy until last week when her blood pressure was first noted to be 142/88 mmHg, is brought to the birth suite by her partner after a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure at home lasting two minutes followed by a period of unresponsiveness. She has had a worsening frontal headache, blurred vision and epigastric pain for 24 hours. On arrival she is post-ictal but rousable to voice. Her blood pressure is 175/115 mmHg and 172/110 mmHg on repeat 5 minutes apart with an appropriately sized cuff. She has 3+ proteinuria on dipstick, mild peripheral oedema and hyperreflexia with three beats of clonus. There is no focal neurology, no neck stiffness and no fever, making other intracranial causes less likely. Foetal heart rate on CTG is 145 bpm with reduced variability and no decelerations. The senior registrar is in the room, the anaesthetist and obstetric consultant are being paged, an IV line is in situ and bloods are being drawn for urgent FBC, coagulation, U&E and LFTs.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 175/115 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, RR 18 /min, SpO2 96% on 4 L O2, GCS 13 (E3V4M6).
data_table.Examination: Post-ictal, drowsy but rousable. Reflexes 4+ with 3 beats clonus. Tongue laterally bitten.
data_table.Investigations: Urine dipstick: protein 3+, blood trace. Bedside Hb 116 g/L, platelets pending. CTG: baseline 145, reduced variability, no decelerations.

lead_in: Which sequence of immediate management steps is most appropriate?

options.text: Immediate caesarean section under general anaesthesia before commencing any magnesium sulphate or antihypertensive therapy at all
options.rationale: Maternal stabilisation with magnesium and blood pressure control must precede delivery; rushing to theatre without stabilisation worsens maternal and foetal outcomes (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: IV diazepam 10 mg as the first-line anticonvulsant, then a phenytoin loading dose, then expedite delivery in theatre
options.rationale: Magnesium sulphate is superior to both diazepam and phenytoin for eclampsia (Magpie trial; Cochrane) and is the first-line drug. Benzodiazepines are reserved for refractory seizures (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Sublingual nifedipine to rapidly reduce blood pressure, then immediate transfer directly to theatre without anticonvulsant cover at all
options.rationale: Sublingual nifedipine causes precipitous, uncontrolled drops in blood pressure with risk of placental hypoperfusion and is not recommended; oral nifedipine SR or IV labetalol/hydralazine is preferred (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Left lateral with oxygen, magnesium sulphate 4 g IV over 15 minutes then 1 g/h, labetalol or hydralazine, then deliver
options.rationale: This is the standard SOMANZ/RANZCOG sequence: left lateral position to reduce aortocaval compression and aspiration risk, airway/oxygen, magnesium loading and maintenance, antihypertensive control aiming for systolic <160 and diastolic <110, then expedited delivery (vaginal or caesarean) once mother is stable (SOMANZ 2014; RANZCOG 2023). Full SOMANZ sequence: airway-resuscitation, magnesium 4 g load then 1 g/h maintenance, IV labetalol or hydralazine for severe hypertension, delivery once stabilised.

options.text: Continuous EEG monitoring at the bedside before commencing any anticonvulsant therapy or antihypertensive medication in this setting
options.rationale: EEG is not required to diagnose or treat eclampsia in this clinical setting; treatment is initiated on clinical grounds (SOMANZ 2014).

explanation.summary: Eclampsia management priorities (mother first, then foetus): left lateral, ABC, magnesium sulphate load and maintenance, control severe hypertension, then expedite delivery. Magnesium is first-line, not benzodiazepines.
key_points: Mother before foetus: stabilise then deliver
Magnesium 4 g IV load, 1 g/h maintenance
BP targets: SBP <160, DBP <110 with IV labetalol/hydralazine or oral nifedipine SR
Continue magnesium for 24 h after delivery or last seizure
Avoid sublingual nifedipine and diazepam first-line
explanation.pearls: Up to one third of eclamptic seizures occur postpartum, most within 48 hours; magnesium continues for 24 h post-delivery.

stem: A 31-year-old G3P2 woman at 35 weeks and 2 days gestation presents with three days of progressively worsening right upper quadrant pain, nausea, and increasing fatigue. She has not had a seizure but has a mild bifrontal headache and no visual scotomata. Her two previous pregnancies were uncomplicated and she has no personal or family history of thrombotic microangiopathy, autoimmune disease or liver disease. Her blood pressure on three readings is 146-150 / 90-94 mmHg, she has 1+ proteinuria with a urine PCR of 42 mg/mmol, and the foetal CTG is reactive with normal growth on a scan one week ago. She has had no recent gastroenteritis, no bloody diarrhoea and no recent E. coli O157:H7 or shigatoxin-producing organism exposure. Bedside bloods show haemoglobin 96 g/L (anaemia with reticulocytosis), platelets 68 x10^9/L, ALT 285 U/L, AST 312 U/L, total bilirubin 38 micromol/L (predominantly indirect), LDH 850 U/L, fibrinogen 3.2 g/L (preserved), INR 1.1, creatinine 84 micromol/L (normal for pregnancy), glucose 5.2 mmol/L, and ammonia within the normal range. A peripheral blood film shows schistocytes, polychromasia and absent platelet clumps. ADAMTS13 activity measured urgently is 65% (well above the 10% threshold for TTP).

data_table.Vital signs: BP 148/92 mmHg, HR 96 bpm, T 36.9 degC.
data_table.Examination: Mild scleral icterus, RUQ tenderness. Uterus appropriate for dates, soft.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 96 g/L, platelets 68 x10^9/L, schistocytes on film, LDH 850 U/L, bilirubin 38 micromol/L (mostly indirect), ALT 285 U/L, AST 312 U/L, INR 1.1, fibrinogen 3.2 g/L, creatinine 84 micromol/L, glucose 5.2 mmol/L, ammonia normal, ADAMTS13 65%.

lead_in: Which diagnosis best fits this clinical and biochemical picture?

options.text: HELLP syndrome (haemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, low platelets)
options.rationale: HELLP is diagnosed by Haemolysis (anaemia, schistocytes, raised LDH, raised indirect bilirubin), Elevated Liver enzymes (ALT/AST >70 U/L typically) and Low Platelets (<100 x10^9/L), usually with normal coagulation and normal glucose. This patient has all three features with preserved fibrinogen, normal INR, normal glucose and ADAMTS13 >10%, distinguishing it from AFLP, TTP and HUS (RANZCOG 2023; SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy with hepatic dysfunction
options.rationale: AFLP typically causes hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy (raised INR, low fibrinogen), hyperammonaemia and more pronounced hyperbilirubinaemia. This patient has normal glucose, normal INR and fibrinogen and normal ammonia (RCOG; BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice - HELLP, AFLP, TTP, HUS

options.text: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura with neurological features
options.rationale: TTP is defined by ADAMTS13 activity <10%, with severe thrombocytopenia, microangiopathic haemolysis, neurological features and fever; ADAMTS13 of 65% effectively excludes TTP (BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice - HELLP, AFLP, TTP, HUS

options.text: Atypical haemolytic uraemic syndrome with complement-mediated injury
options.rationale: HUS is dominated by acute kidney injury and microangiopathic haemolysis with relatively preserved transaminases; this patient has normal creatinine and very high transaminases, making HUS unlikely (BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice - HELLP, AFLP, TTP, HUS

options.text: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy with raised bile acids
options.rationale: ICP causes pruritus and isolated raised bile acids and modest transaminitis without thrombocytopenia or haemolysis (RANZCOG ICP statement).

explanation.summary: HELLP, AFLP, TTP and HUS can all present with thrombocytopenia and microangiopathic features in late pregnancy. Discriminators: HELLP has high LFTs with normal coags and glucose; AFLP has hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy and hyperammonaemia; TTP has ADAMTS13 <10%; HUS is renal-predominant.
key_points: HELLP: Hb low, platelets <100, AST/ALT >70, LDH >600, INR normal, glucose normal
AFLP: hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy, hyperammonaemia, AKI
TTP: ADAMTS13 <10%, severe thrombocytopenia, neuro features
HUS: AKI dominant, often post-diarrhoeal (STEC) or complement-mediated
Mississippi classification grades HELLP severity by platelet count
explanation.pearls: When in doubt, send fibrinogen, glucose, ammonia and ADAMTS13 - they discriminate HELLP from its mimics within hours.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice - HELLP, AFLP, TTP, HUS

subtopic_detail: HELLP vs AFLP vs TTP vs HUS in pregnancy

stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 woman attends for her routine 26-week screening visit. Her booking BMI was 27, she has no family history of diabetes, no previous GDM and no PCOS. She undergoes a 75 g oral glucose tolerance test as part of universal screening at 24-28 weeks gestation in line with Australian (ADIPS) recommendations. The midwife receives the laboratory result and asks the registrar to confirm the venous plasma glucose thresholds that define gestational diabetes mellitus on a 75 g OGTT in Australia. Her result will need to be compared against the ADIPS 2014 diagnostic cut-offs at fasting, 1 hour and 2 hours.
lead_in: Which set of 75 g OGTT venous plasma glucose thresholds defines gestational diabetes in Australia (ADIPS)?

options.text: Fasting >=5.5, 1 h >=10.0, 2 h >=8.5 mmol/L
options.rationale: The fasting threshold is 5.1 mmol/L, not 5.5; 5.5 was the older Australasian Diabetes in Pregnancy Society pre-2014 threshold (ADIPS 2014).

options.text: Fasting >=5.1, 1 h >=10.0, 2 h >=8.5 mmol/L
options.rationale: The ADIPS 2014 (HAPO-derived, IADPSG-aligned) diagnostic thresholds for GDM on a 75 g OGTT are fasting >=5.1, 1-hour >=10.0 and 2-hour >=8.5 mmol/L; one or more abnormal values is sufficient for diagnosis (ADIPS 2014; RANZCOG 2023).

options.text: Fasting >=5.1, 1 h >=10.0, 2 h >=7.8 mmol/L
options.rationale: 2-hour >=7.8 mmol/L is the WHO non-pregnant impaired glucose tolerance threshold, not the GDM threshold (ADIPS 2014; WHO).
These are the diagnostic thresholds for diabetes mellitus outside pregnancy (WHO), much higher than the GDM cut-offs (ADIPS 2014).

options.text: Fasting >=4.5, 1 h >=9.0, 2 h >=7.5 mmol/L
options.rationale: These values are below the ADIPS thresholds and are not used in any major guideline (ADIPS 2014).

explanation.summary: Australia uses ADIPS 2014 (IADPSG-aligned) criteria: 75 g OGTT at 24-28 weeks for universal screening. Any one value at or above fasting 5.1, 1 h 10.0 or 2 h 8.5 mmol/L makes the diagnosis.
key_points: Universal OGTT at 24-28 weeks
Early OGTT in first or early second trimester if high risk (previous GDM, BMI >=30, age >=40, ethnicity, family hx, PCOS, prior macrosomia)
One abnormal value is enough
Repeat 75 g OGTT 6-12 weeks postpartum
explanation.pearls: Memorise 5.1 / 10.0 / 8.5 - they are the only three numbers you need for diagnosis in Australia.

subtopic_detail: Gestational diabetes - ADIPS 75 g OGTT thresholds

stem: A 33-year-old G2P1 woman at 30 weeks and 2 days gestation was diagnosed with GDM at 26 weeks (fasting 5.4 mmol/L, 2 h 9.1 mmol/L on 75 g OGTT). She has been on structured dietary modification with a credentialled dietitian and 30 minutes of daily walking for four weeks. Her four-times-daily capillary glucose diary now shows fasting values consistently 5.6-6.2 mmol/L (ADIPS target <5.1 mmol/L) and 2-hour postprandial values 5.8-6.8 mmol/L (target <6.7 mmol/L) after all three meals. There is no overnight hypoglycaemia, no symptomatic hypoglycaemia, no glycosuria and no ketonuria on bedside testing. Foetal ultrasound at 30 weeks shows estimated foetal weight on the 78th centile with appropriate liquor and normal umbilical and middle cerebral artery Dopplers. She has no contraindication to insulin or metformin, her renal function is normal (creatinine 58 micromol/L), and she has good health literacy and dexterity for self-injection. Her booking BMI was 31 and she has gained 6 kg in pregnancy.

data_table.Investigations: BSL diary: fasting 5.6-6.2; postprandial within target after all meals. HbA1c 5.6%. Renal function normal.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Commence basal-bolus insulin with rapid-acting analogue at meals plus bedtime NPH
options.rationale: Full basal-bolus is reserved for patients with both elevated fasting and postprandial values. This patient has isolated elevated fasting glucose only, so basal insulin alone is sufficient (ADIPS 2014; RANZCOG 2023).

options.text: Commence rapid-acting prandial insulin (aspart or lispro) before each meal only, no basal
options.rationale: Prandial insulin would not address the predominant fasting hyperglycaemia and would risk daytime hypoglycaemia (ADIPS 2014).

options.text: Continue lifestyle measures only and review fasting glucose again in four weeks
options.rationale: Fasting glucose remains above target despite four weeks of optimised lifestyle; pharmacotherapy is indicated to reduce risk of macrosomia and stillbirth (ADIPS 2014).

options.text: Commence empagliflozin 10 mg orally daily as monotherapy in pregnancy
options.rationale: SGLT2 inhibitors are contraindicated in pregnancy due to teratogenicity concerns and lack of safety data (TGA; AMH).

options.text: Commence isophane (NPH) insulin at bedtime, titrated to fasting capillary glucose target
options.rationale: For isolated fasting hyperglycaemia in GDM, the ADIPS-recommended first-line pharmacotherapy is bedtime intermediate-acting (isophane/NPH) insulin titrated to achieve fasting glucose <5.1 mmol/L. Metformin is an acceptable alternative or adjunct in suitable patients but insulin is the established first-line agent (ADIPS 2014; RANZCOG 2023).

explanation.summary: When lifestyle fails in GDM, pharmacotherapy is matched to the pattern of hyperglycaemia: isolated fasting hyperglycaemia -> bedtime isophane (NPH); isolated postprandial -> prandial rapid-acting insulin; both -> basal-bolus regimen. Metformin is an option after counselling about placental transfer.
key_points: ADIPS targets: fasting <5.1, 1 h <7.4, 2 h <6.7 mmol/L
Isolated fasting hyperglycaemia: bedtime NPH first
Isolated postprandial: prandial rapid-acting analogue
Both: basal-bolus
Metformin: alternative or adjunct; crosses placenta; not for severe hyperglycaemia
SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 agonists: contraindicated in pregnancy
explanation.pearls: Match the insulin to the glucose pattern. Don't reach for basal-bolus when only the fasting value is high.

subtopic_detail: GDM - insulin titration after lifestyle failure

stem: A 29-year-old G1P0 woman at 28 weeks gestation has had three antenatal visits over a fortnight with persistently elevated blood pressure readings (142/92, 146/94 and today 144/90 mmHg on repeated measurement with an appropriately sized cuff after five minutes of rest). There is no proteinuria on dipstick, urinary PCR is 12 mg/mmol, full blood count, electrolytes, urate and transaminases are within normal limits, and her foetal growth is on the 50th centile with normal umbilical artery and middle cerebral artery Dopplers. She is asymptomatic with no headache, no visual disturbance and no abdominal pain. She has no history of asthma, no heart block, no significant bradycardia, no liver disease, no known drug allergies and is not taking any over-the-counter medications. She is also currently breastfeeding her partner's older child as a tandem feed (she is a wet-nurse for a friend), so the consultant reminds the registrar that the choice of first-line oral antihypertensive should be both safe in pregnancy and compatible with lactation.

data_table.Examination: Uncomplicated. No oedema. Fundus appropriate for dates.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 122 g/L, platelets 240 x10^9/L, creatinine 62 micromol/L, ALT 22 U/L, urine PCR <30 mg/mmol.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line oral antihypertensive in this Australian setting?

options.text: Labetalol 100 mg orally twice daily, titrated to target BP
options.rationale: Labetalol is the SOMANZ/RANZCOG recommended first-line oral antihypertensive in pregnancy. It is safe in lactation and has the strongest evidence base. Starting dose 100 mg BD, up to 400 mg TDS (SOMANZ 2014; RANZCOG 2023).

options.text: Methyldopa 250 mg orally three times daily, titrated to BP
options.rationale: Methyldopa is a second-line agent in current Australian practice due to slower onset, sedation and mood effects, and the risk of postpartum depression with continued use; labetalol is preferred first-line (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Slow-release nifedipine 20 mg orally twice daily, titrated to BP
options.rationale: Nifedipine SR is an effective second-line oral agent in pregnancy. While reasonable, labetalol remains first-line per SOMANZ unless contraindicated (e.g. asthma, heart block) (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Oral hydralazine 25 mg three times daily, titrated to BP
options.rationale: Oral hydralazine is rarely used as monotherapy due to reflex tachycardia and lupus-like syndrome; IV hydralazine has a role in acute severe hypertension (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Enalapril 5 mg orally once daily, titrated to BP
options.rationale: ACE inhibitors are contraindicated in the second and third trimesters due to foetal renal damage, oligohydramnios and skull hypoplasia (TGA; AMH).

explanation.summary: First-line oral antihypertensives for non-severe hypertension in pregnancy in Australia: labetalol > nifedipine SR > methyldopa. ACE inhibitors and ARBs are contraindicated. Treatment target BP 110-140 / 80-90 mmHg per SOMANZ.
key_points: Labetalol 100-400 mg BD-TDS (first-line)
Nifedipine SR 20-60 mg BD (second-line)
Methyldopa 250-500 mg TDS-QID (second/third-line)
ACE inhibitors and ARBs: contraindicated
Acute severe BP: IV labetalol or IV hydralazine or oral nifedipine SR
explanation.pearls: Avoid labetalol if the patient has asthma, heart block, or significant bradycardia; switch to nifedipine SR.

stem: A 30-year-old G1P0 woman at 28 weeks and 3 days gestation presents to the obstetric assessment unit with a three-day history of fever to 38.6 degC, generalised myalgia, frontal headache and mild non-bloody diarrhoea. She reports having eaten unpasteurised soft cheese from a delicatessen at a friend's gathering one week earlier and recalls that several other guests have since had similar mild symptoms. There is no rash, no respiratory symptoms, no neck stiffness, no urinary symptoms and no contact with anyone with a confirmed infectious diagnosis. Foetal movements remain normal and CTG is reactive with a baseline of 145 bpm. Blood cultures are taken before any antibiotics are commenced and a urine culture is sent. The microbiology registrar advises that pending blood cultures, empirical therapy should cover the most likely food-borne organism in this setting. She has no penicillin allergy, no other drug allergies, normal renal function and is not taking any other medication beyond pregnancy supplements.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.6 degC, BP 110/72 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, RR 18 /min.
data_table.Examination: Looks unwell but not septic. Chest clear. Abdomen soft. Uterus non-tender, fundus appropriate.
data_table.Investigations: WCC 14.5 x10^9/L (neutrophilia), CRP 92 mg/L, blood cultures taken. CTG reactive.

lead_in: Which empirical antimicrobial regimen is most appropriate?

options.text: Ceftriaxone 2 g IV daily as monotherapy without added cover
options.rationale: Listeria monocytogenes is intrinsically resistant to all cephalosporins; ceftriaxone has no useful activity against Listeria (Therapeutic Guidelines; eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 1 g three times daily without IV cover
options.rationale: Oral amoxicillin is inadequate for invasive listeriosis with bacteraemia in pregnancy; IV therapy with high-dose ampicillin (or benzylpenicillin) plus gentamicin synergy is required (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Trimethoprim-sulphamethoxazole IV at renal-dose adjusted intervals as monotherapy without ampicillin
options.rationale: Co-trimoxazole is the preferred agent only for penicillin-allergic patients with listeriosis; the sulpha component is also relatively contraindicated near term due to kernicterus risk (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Ampicillin or benzylpenicillin 2 g IV 6-hourly plus gentamicin 4-5 mg/kg IV daily
options.rationale: Suspected listeriosis in pregnancy with fever after high-risk food exposure is treated empirically with high-dose IV ampicillin or benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin for synergy, pending blood cultures (Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic; RANZCOG; CDNA).
source_refs: CDNA Series of National Guidelines - Listeriosis

options.text: Vancomycin 25 mg/kg IV loading dose then weight-based maintenance infusion as monotherapy
options.rationale: Vancomycin has variable efficacy against Listeria and is not first-line; ampicillin plus gentamicin is preferred (Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Listeria monocytogenes is a food-borne Gram-positive rod (intracellular) acquired from unpasteurised dairy, soft cheese, deli meats, smoked seafood and pre-prepared salads. Pregnancy increases susceptibility 10-20 fold. Treatment is IV ampicillin (or benzylpenicillin) plus gentamicin.
key_points: Cephalosporins do NOT cover Listeria
Empirical Rx: ampicillin 2 g IV q6h + gentamicin 4-5 mg/kg/day
Duration: 2-3 weeks (longer if meningitis)
Penicillin-allergic: co-trimoxazole
Notify public health (CDNA SoNG)
Foetal complications: stillbirth, preterm labour, neonatal sepsis, granulomatosis infantiseptica
explanation.pearls: Any febrile pregnant woman with a high-risk food history needs blood cultures and listeria-active empirical antibiotics before culture results return.

sources.label: CDNA Series of National Guidelines - Listeriosis

stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 woman at 11 weeks and 2 days gestation has a routine booking infection screen which shows Toxoplasma gondii IgM positive and IgG positive with low avidity, a pattern strongly suggesting recent primary infection within the last 12-16 weeks. She has had a new indoor kitten at home since six weeks before conception and recalls cleaning the litter tray on multiple occasions without gloves. She also recalls eating undercooked lamb skewers at a barbecue 10 weeks ago. She is asymptomatic, has no lymphadenopathy on examination and her partner is well with no symptoms. Toxoplasma PCR on maternal blood is positive. . Her booking ultrasound today shows a viable singleton with normal anatomy for gestation and no calcifications. She is referred to maternal-foetal medicine to discuss treatment options pending foetal status, and asks specifically whether the medication chosen now should change if foetal infection is later confirmed on amniotic fluid PCR. The maternal-foetal medicine consultant also outlines the typical vertical transmission risk by trimester and explains why the timing of investigations matters, before turning to the choice of initial pharmacotherapy.

data_table.Investigations: Toxoplasma IgM positive, IgG positive low avidity, maternal blood PCR positive. Booking ultrasound: viable singleton at 11 weeks, normal anatomy for gestation.

lead_in: Which initial pharmacological strategy is most appropriate?

options.text: Spiramycin 1 g orally three times daily, switching to sulphadiazine-pyrimethamine-folinic acid only if amniocentesis confirms foetal infection
options.rationale: Spiramycin is the first-trimester treatment of choice as it concentrates in the placenta and reduces vertical transmission without causing foetal harm. If foetal infection is confirmed (positive amniotic fluid PCR after 18 weeks), therapy switches to sulphadiazine + pyrimethamine + folinic acid to treat established foetal infection (RANZCOG; BMJ Best Practice; RCOG). Spiramycin reduces vertical transmission; sulphadiazine-pyrimethamine-folinic acid is reserved for confirmed foetal infection by amniotic fluid PCR at >=18 weeks.

options.text: Sulphadiazine plus pyrimethamine plus folinic acid commenced immediately for all women regardless of foetal status
options.rationale: Pyrimethamine is a folate antagonist and is teratogenic in the first trimester (neural tube defects, oral clefts); it should be avoided before 14-18 weeks. Treatment of the foetus is reserved for confirmed foetal infection (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Co-trimoxazole oral monotherapy without folinic acid or any foetal assessment as the definitive treatment
options.rationale: Co-trimoxazole has activity against Toxoplasma but is not standard first-line therapy in pregnancy; spiramycin is preferred to prevent transmission (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Azithromycin 500 mg orally daily as definitive monotherapy without spiramycin, folinic acid or referral
options.rationale: Azithromycin has weak in vitro activity but is not a recommended first-line agent in pregnancy (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Termination of pregnancy without any treatment given certain severe congenital toxoplasmosis at this gestation
options.rationale: Maternal primary infection in the first trimester carries a relatively low vertical transmission risk (10-15%) although consequences if transmitted can be severe; treatment with spiramycin substantially reduces transmission. Termination is not the default management (RCOG; BMJ Best Practice).

explanation.summary: Suspected maternal toxoplasmosis is confirmed by serology (IgG avidity, IgM) and maternal PCR. Spiramycin reduces transmission without teratogenicity. Confirmed foetal infection (amniotic PCR after 18 weeks) is treated with sulphadiazine + pyrimethamine + folinic acid.
key_points: Spiramycin: first-line in pregnancy to prevent transmission (does not cross placenta well)
Sulphadiazine + pyrimethamine + folinic acid: for confirmed foetal infection from 14-18 weeks
Folinic acid (leucovorin) protects against pyrimethamine-induced bone marrow toxicity
Diagnose primary infection with low IgG avidity within 16-20 weeks of seroconversion
Vertical transmission risk: T1 ~10-15%, T2 ~30%, T3 ~60% - but severity decreases with gestation
Sources: cat faeces, raw/undercooked meat, unwashed produce, soil
explanation.pearls: Earlier infection equals lower transmission but more severe disease if transmitted; later infection equals higher transmission but milder disease.

stem: A 29-year-old G2P1 woman at 14 weeks and 1 day gestation works as a long-day-care educator caring for children aged 12-24 months and presents to her GP with a fortnight of mild fatigue, low-grade fever, headache, mild cervical lymphadenopathy and arthralgia. She has no rash, no respiratory symptoms and no jaundice. Routine TORCH testing shows CMV IgM positive, CMV IgG positive with low avidity (consistent with infection within the past three months). Critically, her booking CMV IgG four weeks earlier was negative, providing serological evidence of seroconversion in early pregnancy. A maternal blood CMV PCR is positive at a moderate viral load. She has no prior infectious diseases history, no immunosuppression, and her previous pregnancy was uncomplicated with a healthy term infant. She asks the maternal-foetal medicine consultant about the difference between primary and non-primary (reactivation or reinfection) maternal CMV infection in pregnancy, the next investigation step including foetal surveillance and timing of any amniocentesis, and the role and evidence base for any antiviral therapy in pregnancy.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6 degC, BP 110/72 mmHg, HR 84 bpm.
data_table.Examination: Bilateral non-tender cervical lymphadenopathy; otherwise unremarkable.
data_table.Investigations: CMV IgG positive low avidity, IgM positive, blood PCR positive. Booking CMV IgG (4 weeks prior): negative. LFTs mildly raised (ALT 65). Hb 122 g/L.

lead_in: Which statement most accurately describes the current Australian approach to maternal CMV infection in this case?

options.text: Non-primary CMV infection carries a higher vertical transmission rate than primary infection, so reassurance is appropriate here
options.rationale: Primary maternal CMV has a substantially higher vertical transmission rate (~30-40%) and a higher rate of severe sequelae than non-primary infection (~1-2% transmission, lower severity) (RANZCOG; BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice - CMV in pregnancy

options.text: Maternal intravenous ganciclovir is the established standard of care to prevent foetal CMV infection in primary seroconversion
options.rationale: IV ganciclovir is teratogenic in animal studies and is not routinely used in pregnancy; valaciclovir or valganciclovir trials have shown some benefit but no agent is universally recommended (BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice - CMV in pregnancy

options.text: Spiramycin reduces vertical transmission of CMV in the same way that it does for Toxoplasma gondii
options.rationale: Spiramycin has no role in CMV management; it is specific to toxoplasmosis (BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice - CMV in pregnancy

options.text: CMV is a rare cause of congenital infection and routine foetal surveillance is unnecessary after maternal seroconversion
options.rationale: Congenital CMV is the most common congenital infection in developed countries (around 0.5-1% of live births) and the leading non-genetic cause of sensorineural hearing loss; serial foetal ultrasound and consideration of amniotic PCR after 21 weeks are recommended (RANZCOG).

options.text: Serial foetal ultrasound, amniocentesis for CMV PCR after 21 weeks and 6 weeks post-seroconversion, and high-dose valaciclovir following MFM discussion
options.rationale: This reflects the current RANZCOG/RCOG/BMJ Best Practice approach: confirm primary infection (low avidity, seroconversion), serial foetal ultrasound, amniotic PCR after 21 weeks and at least 6 weeks post-seroconversion to maximise sensitivity, and consider maternal high-dose valaciclovir 8 g/day which a randomised trial showed reduces vertical transmission (RANZCOG; BMJ Best Practice; Shahar-Nissan 2020). Surveillance includes brain biometry and growth; amniocentesis sensitivity rises after 21 weeks and 6 weeks post-seroconversion; high-dose valaciclovir is the only intervention with RCT-supported reduction in vertical transmission.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice - CMV in pregnancy

explanation.summary: Congenital CMV is the most common congenital infection and the leading non-genetic cause of childhood sensorineural hearing loss. Primary maternal infection transmits in 30-40% of cases; non-primary in 1-2%. Management includes serial foetal ultrasound, amniotic PCR after 21 weeks, and consideration of maternal high-dose valaciclovir.
key_points: Primary infection: 30-40% transmission; non-primary: 1-2%
Diagnose by IgG seroconversion or low IgG avidity + IgM positive
Amniotic PCR: after 21 weeks AND >=6 weeks post seroconversion
Foetal US findings: ventriculomegaly, periventricular calcifications, hyperechogenic bowel, IUGR, hepatosplenomegaly
Valaciclovir 8 g/day may reduce vertical transmission (RCT 2020)
No effective vaccine; childcare hand hygiene is key prevention
explanation.pearls: Maternal CMV is often clinically silent; hearing screening at birth is critical for affected neonates.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice - CMV in pregnancy

subtopic_detail: CMV in pregnancy - primary vs non-primary infection

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 woman at 24 weeks and 2 days gestation with a background of mild persistent asthma on budesonide-formoterol maintenance (one inhalation twice daily as preventer plus reliever) presents to the emergency department with 12 hours of progressive wheeze, dyspnoea and chest tightness after a viral upper respiratory infection. She is too breathless to speak in full sentences. Her best personal PEFR is 420 L/min; in ED it is measured at 210 L/min (50% of personal best). SpO2 is 93% on room air. Auscultation reveals widespread polyphonic expiratory wheeze with reduced air entry bilaterally and no focal crackles or signs of consolidation. She is afebrile at 37.1 degC, has no calf swelling, no chest pain, the foetus is moving normally and the CTG is reactive with a baseline of 140 bpm. She has stopped her preventer inhaler for the past four days because she was worried about possible effects on the baby and asks whether she should continue inhaled corticosteroids and whether oral prednisolone is safe in pregnancy, given a friend has told her to refuse steroids.

data_table.Vital signs: RR 28 /min, HR 118 bpm, SpO2 93% RA, BP 124/78 mmHg, T 37.1 degC.
data_table.Examination: Speaking in short phrases, accessory muscle use, widespread polyphonic wheeze. CTG reactive.
data_table.Investigations: PEFR 210 L/min (50% of personal best 420). ABG not yet performed. CXR not indicated initially.

lead_in: Which management approach is most appropriate?

options.text: Continue maintenance inhaled corticosteroid but withhold all systemic corticosteroids in pregnancy due to perceived foetal teratogenic risk
options.rationale: Oral prednisolone is safe in pregnancy and is essential treatment for a moderate/severe asthma exacerbation; withholding it risks maternal and foetal hypoxia, which is far more harmful than transient corticosteroid exposure (Therapeutic Guidelines; National Asthma Council).
source_refs: National Asthma Council - Australian Asthma Handbook (Pregnancy)

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol with ipratropium if severe, continue ICS, oral prednisolone 37.5-50 mg for 5-10 days, and controlled oxygen to SpO2 94-98%
options.rationale: This is the standard exacerbation algorithm. Salbutamol bronchodilation, oxygen to target SpO2 94-98% (95% to maintain foetal oxygenation), maintenance ICS continued, oral prednisolone 37.5-50 mg daily for short course are all safe and indicated. Maternal hypoxia harms the foetus far more than these medications (National Asthma Council; Therapeutic Guidelines; RANZCOG). ICS and oral prednisolone are safe in pregnancy and reassurance about safety is part of the management.
source_refs: National Asthma Council - Australian Asthma Handbook (Pregnancy)

options.text: Switch the patient to a leukotriene receptor antagonist (oral montelukast) alone as monotherapy maintenance throughout pregnancy
options.rationale: LTRAs (e.g. montelukast) are adjuncts, not first-line, and have no role in acute exacerbation management (National Asthma Council).
source_refs: National Asthma Council - Australian Asthma Handbook (Pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulphate 2 g over 20 minutes given as the immediate first-line therapy in pregnancy
options.rationale: IV magnesium is reserved for severe exacerbations refractory to initial bronchodilator and corticosteroid therapy, not first-line (National Asthma Council).
source_refs: National Asthma Council - Australian Asthma Handbook (Pregnancy)

options.text: Discharge home with a short-acting beta-2 agonist alone once PEFR has reached 60% of personal best
options.rationale: PEFR 60% of best with a recent severe exacerbation does not meet safe discharge criteria; the patient needs systemic corticosteroids, observation and a written asthma action plan (National Asthma Council).
source_refs: National Asthma Council - Australian Asthma Handbook (Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Acute asthma in pregnancy is treated identically to non-pregnant patients with the additional priority of avoiding maternal hypoxia. Continue ICS, give salbutamol and ipratropium, oral prednisolone for moderate/severe exacerbations, and supplemental oxygen.
key_points: Maintenance ICS: safe and essential in pregnancy
Salbutamol: safe; first-line bronchodilator
Oral prednisolone 37.5-50 mg daily for 5-10 days for exacerbation
Target SpO2 94-98% (or >=95% in pregnancy)
IV magnesium for severe refractory cases
Step up therapy if severity criteria escalate; involve obstetric medicine
explanation.pearls: Maternal asthma control is more important than any medication risk; uncontrolled asthma causes IUGR, preterm birth and pre-eclampsia.

sources.label: National Asthma Council - Australian Asthma Handbook (Pregnancy)

stem: A 28-year-old G2P0 woman with focal-onset epilepsy attends a joint obstetric-medicine/neurology pre-conception clinic three months before a planned pregnancy. She has been seizure-free for two years on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily monotherapy, having previously failed levetiracetam (rash and breakthrough seizures) and carbamazepine (breakthrough seizures with intolerable somnolence) for seizure control. Her previous pregnancy ended in a first-trimester miscarriage while she was on lamotrigine monotherapy two years ago, and she has not used lamotrigine since. She has no family history of neural tube defects or congenital malformations and her BMI is 23. She is concerned about teratogenicity having read media coverage of valproate, and asks specifically about: relative major congenital malformation rates of the commonly used AEDs, the appropriate periconceptional folate dose for women on AEDs versus the general population, the likely need for therapeutic drug monitoring during pregnancy (particularly for lamotrigine if used), and which medication she should ideally be established on by the time of conception. Her neurologist has reviewed her recent MRI and EEG which remain stable, and confirms she has reliable epilepsy without evidence of progressive structural lesion. She does not drive a heavy vehicle for work and has well-controlled mood with no current depressive symptoms. She is keen to plan a pregnancy within the next six months and is willing to delay conception if a medication switch is recommended, but wants a clear plan that balances seizure control against teratogenic risk and includes the practical issue of how to monitor any new agent through pregnancy. She also asks whether to expect a dose adjustment in pregnancy and what to do about breastfeeding postpartum, and her partner asks whether she should stop driving during the cross-titration period as a safety precaution.

data_table.Investigations: Recent EEG and MRI stable. Valproate level within therapeutic range. Renal and hepatic function normal.

lead_in: Which combination of recommendations is most appropriate?

options.text: Continue sodium valproate, commence folate 0.5 mg daily, with no need for AED drug levels during pregnancy
options.rationale: Valproate carries the highest major congenital malformation rate of all AEDs (~10%) plus a 30-40% risk of neurodevelopmental impairment, and is contraindicated in women of childbearing potential unless every alternative has failed. Folate dose for AED users is 5 mg, not 0.5 mg (Therapeutic Goods Administration; AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Switch to phenytoin before pregnancy as it carries the lowest teratogenicity profile of all the AEDs
options.rationale: Phenytoin causes foetal hydantoin syndrome and has a major malformation rate around 5-6%, higher than lamotrigine or levetiracetam (Therapeutic Guidelines; AMH).

options.text: Switch (under neurology) from valproate to lamotrigine or levetiracetam pre-conception, folate 5 mg daily pre-conception, and therapeutic AED level monitoring across pregnancy
options.rationale: Lamotrigine and levetiracetam have the lowest major malformation rates (~2-3%, similar to background). Valproate (~10%) is avoided in women of childbearing potential. Folate 5 mg daily is recommended for women on AEDs. Lamotrigine clearance increases substantially in pregnancy, requiring level monitoring and dose adjustment (Therapeutic Guidelines - Neurology; AMH; RANZCOG; NICE). Switch should be 3-6 months pre-conception; lamotrigine levels fall markedly in pregnancy. Continued AED therapy is essential because uncontrolled seizures cause more foetal harm than well-chosen AEDs.

options.text: Continue valproate, add lamotrigine to permit valproate dose reduction, and use folate 0.5 mg daily throughout
options.rationale: Polytherapy increases teratogenic risk and valproate remains highly teratogenic at any dose; the goal is monotherapy with the safest agent (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Stop all antiepileptic medications before conception entirely to eliminate teratogenic risk to the developing foetus completely
options.rationale: Stopping AEDs in someone with a clear epilepsy diagnosis risks status epilepticus, maternal injury, hypoxia and foetal loss; risk of uncontrolled seizures outweighs medication risk (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Pre-conception epilepsy care: switch from valproate to lamotrigine or levetiracetam (lowest teratogenicity), folate 5 mg daily, plan for AED level monitoring (lamotrigine clearance rises ~2-3 fold), continue therapy because uncontrolled seizures cause more foetal harm than well-chosen AEDs.
key_points: Major malformation rates: lamotrigine ~2.3%, levetiracetam ~2.4%, carbamazepine ~5%, phenytoin ~6%, topiramate ~4-9%, valproate ~10%
Valproate also causes 30-40% neurodevelopmental impairment
Folate 5 mg daily for AED users (vs 0.5 mg general population)
Lamotrigine levels fall in pregnancy; monitor and dose-adjust
Vitamin K 1 mg IM to neonate at birth (standard)
Breastfeeding is safe on most AEDs (lamotrigine, levetiracetam, carbamazepine, valproate)
explanation.pearls: Switch off valproate at least 3-6 months before conception, not once pregnancy is confirmed - by then the teratogenic window is open.

subtopic_detail: Epilepsy in pregnancy - AED selection and teratogenicity

stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 woman is referred to the obstetric medicine clinic at 7 weeks gestation. She was diagnosed with Graves disease six months ago after presenting with tremor, weight loss and palpitations, and is currently on carbimazole 15 mg daily with normal thyroid function tests and a strongly positive TSH-receptor antibody (TRAb 5.2 IU/L). She is asymptomatic, has no goitre and no ophthalmopathy on examination today. She asks about the safest antithyroid medication strategy through pregnancy and what monitoring will be required for both her and the foetus. Her partner has read online that radioactive iodine is curative and asks whether she should have radioactive iodine ablation during pregnancy if her thyroid function deteriorates, and whether the baby is at any specific risk either from her underlying autoimmune condition (TRAb crossing the placenta) or from her antithyroid medication exposure throughout gestation.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 112/68 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: Clinically euthyroid. No tremor. No goitre. No ophthalmopathy.
data_table.Investigations: TSH 1.4 mIU/L, free T4 14 pmol/L, free T3 4.2 pmol/L (all within trimester-specific reference). TRAb 5.2 IU/L (positive).

lead_in: Which management plan is most appropriate?

options.text: Continue carbimazole at the current daily dose throughout pregnancy; radioactive iodine is absolutely contraindicated and the neonate carries no risk
options.rationale: Carbimazole (and its active metabolite methimazole) is associated with embryopathy (aplasia cutis, choanal atresia, oesophageal atresia) in the first trimester; switch to propylthiouracil for T1. Radioactive iodine is correctly identified as contraindicated, but TRAb-positive mothers do confer a neonatal thyrotoxicosis risk (Endocrine Society; RANZCOG).
source_refs: Endocrine Society - Thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum

options.text: Continue carbimazole; defer radioactive iodine until after delivery; neonate has no thyroid risk because the mother is currently euthyroid on therapy
options.rationale: Carbimazole should be switched to PTU in T1 due to embryopathy risk. TRAb positive status confers neonatal thyrotoxicosis risk regardless of maternal thyroid function because antibodies cross the placenta (Endocrine Society).
source_refs: Endocrine Society - Thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum

options.text: Stop all antithyroid medication immediately because pregnancy is naturally immunosuppressive and Graves disease usually remits spontaneously without any pharmacological intervention being required
options.rationale: Stopping antithyroid medication risks maternal thyrotoxicosis and complications including miscarriage, preterm birth, IUGR, pre-eclampsia and foetal/neonatal thyroid dysfunction; continue treatment at the lowest effective dose (Endocrine Society).
source_refs: Endocrine Society - Thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum

options.text: Switch to radioactive iodine ablation now to avoid all antithyroid drug exposure to the developing foetus during this pregnancy and beyond
options.rationale: Radioactive iodine (I-131) is absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy due to foetal thyroid ablation after 10-12 weeks and total body radiation exposure (Endocrine Society).
source_refs: Endocrine Society - Thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum

options.text: PTU in trimester 1 (carbimazole 15 mg ~ PTU 150 mg daily), switch back to carbimazole in T2-T3 due to PTU hepatotoxicity; arrange neonatal TFTs given TRAb
options.rationale: Carbimazole has a higher T1 teratogenicity risk; PTU is preferred in the first trimester despite a small risk of severe hepatotoxicity, which is why guidelines recommend switching back to carbimazole from T2. Radioactive iodine is contraindicated throughout pregnancy. TRAb crosses the placenta and can cause foetal or neonatal thyrotoxicosis; neonatal TFTs are required (Endocrine Society 2017; RANZCOG; ATA). PTU is preferred in T1 due to lower teratogenicity (carbimazole embryopathy); carbimazole preferred T2-T3 because PTU has higher hepatotoxicity risk; TRAb positivity confers neonatal thyrotoxicosis risk requiring neonatal TFT screening at birth and 1-2 weeks.
source_refs: Endocrine Society - Thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum
ATA Guidelines for thyroid disease in pregnancy 2017

explanation.summary: Graves in pregnancy: PTU in T1 (lower embryopathy risk), then carbimazole/methimazole in T2/T3 (lower hepatotoxicity risk). Radioactive iodine is absolutely contraindicated. TRAb positivity confers neonatal thyrotoxicosis risk; neonatal TFTs are mandatory.
key_points: T1: propylthiouracil (PTU)
T2-T3: carbimazole/methimazole (PTU = severe liver injury risk)
Conversion: 1 mg carbimazole ~ 10-20 mg PTU (use 10:1 conventionally)
Target: maternal fT4 at or just above upper limit of trimester-specific range
Radioactive iodine: contraindicated in pregnancy and lactation
TRAb positive: foetal/neonatal thyrotoxicosis risk - neonatal TFTs day 4-7
explanation.pearls: Methimazole/carbimazole embryopathy includes aplasia cutis and a distinctive choanal/oesophageal atresia phenotype; switch off it before or by 6-10 weeks gestation.

sources.label: Endocrine Society - Thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum
ATA Guidelines for thyroid disease in pregnancy 2017

stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 woman with longstanding primary autoimmune hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 100 microg daily attends the antenatal clinic at 6 weeks gestation by certain LMP with a positive home pregnancy test which she did this morning. Her TSH four weeks ago, pre-conception, was 2.0 mIU/L on her current dose and she has been clinically euthyroid. She is asymptomatic with no symptoms of hypothyroidism (no cold intolerance, fatigue, constipation, dry skin or weight change). She takes her levothyroxine on an empty stomach 30 minutes before breakfast and does not take iron or calcium within four hours of her dose. She asks the GP about the dose adjustment to make now (if any), the optimal frequency of thyroid function testing through pregnancy, and the trimester-specific TSH target range she should be aiming for, given that the laboratory reference interval she sees on her result printout (0.4-4.0 mIU/L) is for non-pregnant adults. Her previous pregnancy two years ago was complicated by first-trimester pregnancy loss at 8 weeks when her TSH was found retrospectively to be 6.5 mIU/L early in that pregnancy because she had not increased her dose at conception. She is anti-TPO positive.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/70 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, T 36.6 degC.
data_table.Investigations: Pre-conception TSH (4 weeks ago): 2.0 mIU/L on levothyroxine 100 microg daily. Anti-TPO positive.

lead_in: Which combined management plan most closely matches current guidance (Endocrine Society / ATA / RANZCOG)?

options.text: Continue 100 microg daily, recheck TSH at 28 weeks gestation, target TSH <4.0 mIU/L throughout the entire pregnancy
options.rationale: Dose typically needs to increase by 25-30% in early pregnancy due to increased thyroid hormone requirement; TSH should be checked every 4 weeks until mid-pregnancy, and trimester-specific upper TSH targets are lower than 4.0 (Endocrine Society; ATA).
source_refs: Endocrine Society - Thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum
ATA Guidelines for thyroid disease in pregnancy 2017

options.text: Reduce levothyroxine by 25% because pregnancy is associated with a hyperthyroid physiological state secondary to beta-hCG cross-reactivity
options.rationale: Although beta-hCG can transiently suppress TSH in the first trimester, women with established hypothyroidism on levothyroxine need a DOSE INCREASE of approximately 25-30%, not a reduction, due to higher thyroid-binding globulin and placental transfer (Endocrine Society).
source_refs: Endocrine Society - Thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum

options.text: Cease levothyroxine completely now to assess underlying endogenous thyroid function before any further dose adjustment is made in pregnancy
options.rationale: Stopping levothyroxine risks severe hypothyroidism, miscarriage and impaired neurodevelopment; continue and increase the dose (Endocrine Society).
source_refs: Endocrine Society - Thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum

options.text: Increase levothyroxine 25-30% now (e.g. 125 microg daily), check TSH 4-weekly until 20 weeks then once at 26-32 weeks with trimester-specific target
options.rationale: Endocrine Society/ATA/RANZCOG guidance: known hypothyroid women should immediately increase levothyroxine by ~25-30% on pregnancy confirmation, recheck TSH every 4 weeks until 20 weeks then at least once in T3, targeting trimester-specific TSH (T1 <2.5 mIU/L is the historical target; current ATA recommends using local trimester-specific reference ranges, with an upper limit typically <2.5-3.0 mIU/L). The 9-doses-per-week trick is a practical 28% increase (Endocrine Society 2007/2012; ATA 2017; RANZCOG). Trimester-specific TSH targets are typically <2.5 mIU/L T1, <3.0 mIU/L T2-T3, with lower bound ~0.1 mIU/L.
source_refs: Endocrine Society - Thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum
ATA Guidelines for thyroid disease in pregnancy 2017

options.text: Increase dose by 25-30% but only check TSH if she develops overt clinical symptoms of hypothyroidism in this pregnancy
options.rationale: Symptoms are unreliable in pregnancy; routine 4-weekly TSH monitoring until mid-pregnancy is required because of dynamic dose requirements (Endocrine Society).
source_refs: Endocrine Society - Thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum

explanation.summary: Established hypothyroidism in pregnancy: increase levothyroxine ~25-30% on confirmation of pregnancy, monitor TSH every 4 weeks until 20 weeks then again in third trimester, target trimester-specific TSH (T1 <2.5; T2/T3 <3.0 mIU/L) within the local reference range.
key_points: Immediate ~25-30% dose increase (or 9 doses/week)
TSH every 4 weeks until 20 weeks, then once between 26-32 weeks
Trimester-specific upper limits: T1 ~2.5, T2-T3 ~3.0 mIU/L (use local ranges if available)
Take levothyroxine on empty stomach, 30-60 minutes before food and 4 hours apart from iron/calcium
Postpartum: revert to pre-pregnancy dose and recheck TSH at 6 weeks
Untreated overt hypothyroidism: miscarriage, preterm birth, neurodevelopmental delay
explanation.pearls: Tell women to add two extra tablets per week as soon as they get a positive pregnancy test (9 doses per week) - a practical way to deliver the ~28% increase without waiting for clinic review.

sources.label: Endocrine Society - Thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum
ATA Guidelines for thyroid disease in pregnancy 2017

subtopic_detail: Hypothyroidism in pregnancy - levothyroxine and TSH targets
